message.content: Would you list all the names of existing test suites?

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
The test suites defined in this project are:

| Suite | Tabtarget | Case count |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **fast** | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` | 75 cases |
| **service** | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.service.sh` | 81 cases |
| **crucible** | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.crucible.sh` | 117 cases |
| **complete** | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.complete.sh` | 122 cases |

These are the theurge (RBTW pipeline) suites, tiered by dependency:

- **fast** — no dependencies (enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke)
- **service** — adds GCP credentials (access-probe, hallmark-lifecycle, batch-vouch)
- **crucible** — adds container runtime (tadmor-security, srjcl-jupyter, pluml-diagram)
- **complete** — all 8 fixtures

Want me to verify this against the actual tabtargets in `tt/`, or were you after something broader (e.g., the BUK self-test, qualify gauntlets, or individual fixtures)?

message.content: What about skirmish?

content.text:
Good catch — "skirmish" isn't one of the four theurge test suites I listed. It's a **qualify gauntlet**, which is a different category. There are five qualify levels (the `rbw-t*` colophons), which orchestrate qualification rather than being fixture-grouping test suites:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Purpose |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | Tabtargets, colophons, nameplate health |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | + shellcheck, full test suite |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine | Gauntlet test suite (release gate) |
| **`rbw-tS`** | **QualifySkirmish** | **Mini-gauntlet suite (reuses standing depot, no project churn)** |
| `rbw-tT` | QualifyTadmor | Tadmor self-contained: local kludge build + charge + security cases (no cloud) |

So the distinction is:

- **Test suites** (`rbtd-s.TestSuite.*` — RBTW/theurge pipeline): `fast`, `service`, `crucible`, `complete` — these group test *fixtures* by dependency tier.
- **Qualify gauntlets** (`rbw-t*`): `QualifyFast`, `QualifyRelease`, `QualifyPristine`, `QualifySkirmish`, `QualifyTadmor` — these are release/health qualification orchestrators.

Skirmish is described as a "mini-gauntlet" — a lighter version of the Pristine gauntlet that reuses a standing depot to avoid the cost of provisioning/tearing down GCP project infrastructure on each run.

Want me to dig into what cases or fixtures the skirmish gauntlet actually runs (vs. pristine)? That detail isn't in the CLAUDE.md context, so I'd need to read the relevant source.

message.content: Do we have duplicate tabtargets therefore?

content.text:
No — they're not duplicates, even though the word "fast" appears in both. The naming discipline keeps them in separate namespaces:

- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` — colophon `rbtd-s`, **imprint** `fast`. This is the theurge fixture-suite runner; `fast` is a *parameter* (which suite) baked into the filename. The same colophon `rbtd-s` also carries imprints `service`, `crucible`, `complete`.
- `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` — colophon `rbw-tf`, frontispiece `QualifyFast`. Different colophon, different launcher, different pipeline (RBW workbench, not RBTW theurge). `Fast` here is a human-readable *frontispiece*, not a parameter.

So `fast` plays two structurally different roles: an **imprint** (folio value) under `rbtd-s`, and part of a **frontispiece** under `rbw-tf`. Per the BUK tabtarget grammar (`{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`), the colophon is the routing identity — and `rbtd-s` ≠ `rbw-tf`, so there's no collision.

The real relationship is likely *composition*, not duplication: QualifyRelease/QualifyPristine almost certainly invoke a theurge suite internally. The reused "fast" label is a coincidence of English, not a duplicated launcher.

Want me to actually `ls tt/` and confirm there are no two tabtargets sharing a colophon+imprint (the only thing that would be a true duplicate)?

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#

#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTW Workbench - Routes theurge colophons to RBTE CLI
#
# Pure router: no inline logic, no fixture knowledge, no kindle chains.
# Fully orthogonal from VOW (vvk/jjk/cmk kit pipeline).
# Theurge is Recipe Bottle's own test infrastructure — permanently part of rbk.

set -euo pipefail

RBTW_SCRIPT_DIR="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

buc_context "${0##*/}"

# Source CLI (performs light furnish: buv + burd + buz + rbz + rbte kindle)
source "${RBTW_SCRIPT_DIR}/rbte_cli.sh"

rbtw_main() {
  local z_command="${1:-}"
  shift || true

  test -n "${z_command}" || buc_die "No command specified"

  rbte_dispatch "${z_command}" "$@"
}

rbtw_main "$@"

# eof

content.content:
rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_fixture="${1:-}"
  local z_case="${2:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

# eof

    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"

    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")

  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle

    "enrollment-validation"

    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"

rbq_qualify_fast() {

}

rbq_qualify_release() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running release qualification"

  # Phase 1: Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight)
  rbq_qualify_fast

  # Phase 2: Shellcheck (expensive, release-only)
  buc_step "Running shellcheck"
  buq_shellcheck "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}" \
    "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc" \
    "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt"

  # Phase 3: Complete test suite (sequential)
  buc_step "Running complete test suite"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"

  buc_step "Release qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_pristine() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # The gauntlet's first content fixture (pristine-lifecycle case 1) is the
  # entry-contract gate: tree clean, RBRR blank, RBRA absent, nameplate
  # hallmarks empty, vessel depot fields empty. Preceding enrollment-validation
  # is state-indifferent. The bash for-loop in rbte_suite() runs under set -e
  # so any fixture failure halts the gauntlet immediately.
  buc_step "Running pristine qualification (gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"

  buc_step "Pristine qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_skirmish() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # Skirmish — the mini-gauntlet. Runs the depot->build->crucible chain against
  # a depot the operator has already levied by hand: canonical-invest skips the
  # levy and pristine-lifecycle is dropped, so no GCP project is created per
  # run (cloud build/GAR are still spent). The release gate stays rbw-tP; this
  # is the project-lifecycle-frugal substitute for iterative runs.
  # PRECONDITION: install canonical prefixes + levy a depot (rbw-dL) by hand.
  buc_step "Running skirmish qualification (mini-gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.skirmish.sh"

  buc_step "Skirmish qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_tadmor() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
  buc_step "Building tadmor vessels locally (kludge sentry + bottle, commit hallmarks)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.kludge-tadmor.sh"

  buc_step "Running tadmor crucible fixture (charge + security + quench)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh"

  buc_step "Tadmor self-contained qualification passed"
}

# eof

content.text:
I traced every one to its actual implementation. **No, none of them do the same thing** — they're distinct, and the structure is composition, not duplication. But I found two real defects along the way.

## The two families, fully traced

**Six `rbtd-s.TestSuite.*` tabtargets** — all share one code path (`rbte_suite` in `rbte_engine.sh:160`), differing only by imprint, which selects a genuinely different fixture array (`rbte_engine.sh:42-91`):

| Imprint | Fixtures |
|---------|----------|
| `fast` | enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke, handbook-render, dockerfile-hygiene |
| `service` | fast + access-probe, hallmark-lifecycle, batch-vouch |
| `crucible` | fast + tadmor, srjcl, pluml |
| `complete` | fast + service-extras + crucible-extras |
| `gauntlet` | enrollment-validation, pristine-lifecycle, canonical-establish, onboarding-sequence, regime-validation, regime-smoke, dockerfile-hygiene, hallmark-lifecycle, tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml |
| `skirmish` | gauntlet minus pristine-lifecycle/canonical-establish, plus canonical-invest (reuses standing depot) |

`fast ⊂ service`, `fast ⊂ crucible`, `complete = service ∪ crucible`; `gauntlet` and `skirmish` are a separate release-ladder. All six resolve to distinct sets — no duplication.

**Five `rbw-t*` qualify tabtargets** — each routes to a distinct `rbq_qualify_*` (`rbq_Qualify.sh:151-244`):

| Tabtarget | What it actually does |
|-----------|----------------------|
| `rbw-tf` QualifyFast | **Static checks only** — tabtarget/colophon/context lint + nameplate preflight. *Runs no test fixtures.* |
| `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease | QualifyFast + shellcheck + complete suite |
| `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine | → `rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh` |
| `rbw-tS` QualifySkirmish | → `rbtd-s.TestSuite.skirmish.sh` |
| `rbw-tT` QualifyTadmor | → two `FixtureRun` tabtargets (kludge-tadmor, then tadmor) |

The key disproof of your original "duplicate?" concern: **`QualifyFast` and `TestSuite.fast` are not even similar** — QualifyFast runs zero fixtures (it's a lint/health gate), while the fast suite runs five test fixtures. The shared word "fast" is the only thing they have in common.

## Two defects found

1. **Broken reference in `rbq_qualify_release`** (`rbq_Qualify.sh:182`): it execs `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh`, which **does not exist** — the file is `rbtd-s.TestSuite.complete.sh` (confirmed by `ls`). This is stale from the `rbw-ts` → `rbtd-s` rename; QualifyRelease will fail at its complete-suite phase.

2. **CLAUDE.md is stale**: it documents fast as 3 fixtures / 75 cases, but fast now has 5 fixtures (handbook-render and dockerfile-hygiene were added), and the `gauntlet`/`skirmish` suites aren't in the CLAUDE.md table at all.

Want me to fix the `rbw-ts` → `rbtd-s` reference in `rbq_Qualify.sh:182` and refresh the CLAUDE.md suite table? Both are mechanical; the first is a latent breakage worth a notch.

content.text:
The resolver column came back blank only because my grep ran from `tt/` with a bad relative path — I already read the resolver directly earlier (`rbte_engine.sh:117-122`) and confirmed all six cases (`fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish`) are present. The substantive finding stands.

## Answer: the `rbtd-s` tabtargets (and all 25 `rbtd-*`) are byte-identical files

The `md5sum` confirms it: **all 25 `rbtd-*` tabtargets hash to a single value.** Every one is the exact same three lines:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
export BURD_LAUNCHER=launcher.rbtw_workbench.sh
exec "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/z-launcher.sh" "${0##*/}" "${@}"
```

So in the literal sense, yes — they are duplicate *files*. But that's the whole point of the architecture, not a defect:

- The file content carries **zero** per-target information. It doesn't even contain its own name.
- All routing rides on `${0##*/}` — the runtime basename — which `z-launcher.sh` forwards to the workbench, which parses it into tokens: `BURD_TOKEN_1`=colophon (`rbtd-s`), `BURD_TOKEN_2`=frontispiece (`TestSuite`/`FixtureCase`), `BURD_TOKEN_3`=imprint (`fast`, `gauntlet`, …).
- The **filename is the entire payload.** `rbte_dispatch` keys on token 1, `rbte_suite` keys on token 3.

This is exactly what your recent commit `34c60565e` ("Retired the tabtarget dispatch sprue… exec line byte-identical across all tabtargets") set out to achieve. Before that commit each tabtarget embedded its own dispatch token; now identity is fully externalized to the filename + the `BURD_LAUNCHER` line. The byte-identity is the *success criterion* of that refactor, not a smell.

So the precise distinction for your "duplicate?" question across this whole thread:

| Level | `rbtd-s.TestSuite.*` | Verdict |
|-------|---------------------|---------|
| File bytes | identical (md5 confirms) | "duplicate" — **by design** |
| Dispatch (colophon+frontispiece+imprint) | unique per file | not duplicate |
| Resolved fixture set | unique per imprint | not duplicate |

The only thing that would be a genuine bug is two tabtargets sharing the same *filename tokens* (colophon+frontispiece+imprint) — and there are none; all 25 basenames are distinct.

The one actual breakage remains the stale `rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh` reference in `rbq_Qualify.sh:182`, which should point at the byte-identical-but-correctly-named `rbtd-s.TestSuite.complete.sh`. Want me to fix that line?

=== rbw_workbench.sh (how does it dispatch?) ===
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBW Workbench - Routes Recipe Bottle commands to CLIs via zipper registry
#
# All commands dispatch via buz_exec_lookup (see rbz_zipper.sh for colophon mapping).
# Qualification gate runs for rbw-cC (crucible charge) and rbw-fO (hallmark ordain) before dispatch.

set -euo pipefail

# Get script directory
RBW_SCRIPT_DIR="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

# Source dependencies
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"
source "${RBW_SCRIPT_DIR}/rbz_zipper.sh"

# Show filename on each displayed line
buc_context "${0##*/}"

# Kindle dispatch and zipper registry
zbuv_kindle
zburd_kindle
zbuz_kindle
zrbz_kindle

# Verbose output if BURE_VERBOSE is set
rbw_show() {
  test "${BURE_VERBOSE:-0}" != "1" || echo "RBWSHOW: $*"
}

######################################################################
# Routing

rbw_route() {
  local z_command="$1"
  shift

  rbw_show "Routing command: ${z_command} with args: $*"

  zburd_sentinel
  zrbz_healthcheck

  rbw_show "BURD environment verified"

  # Qualification gate for commands that need it
  case "${z_command}" in
    "${RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE}"|"${RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK}")
      "${RBW_SCRIPT_DIR}/rbq_cli.sh" rbq_qualify_fast || buc_die "Qualification gate failed"
      ;;
  esac

  buz_exec_lookup "${z_command}" "${RBW_SCRIPT_DIR}" "$@"
}

rbw_main() {
  local z_command="${1:-}"
  shift || true

  test -n "${z_command}" || buc_die "No command specified"

  rbw_route "${z_command}" "$@"
}

rbw_main "$@"

# eof

content.text:
Now I can compare the two routing paths precisely. **Same outer spine, different destination and different inner dispatch mechanism.**

## Shared spine (identical for both)

Every tabtarget in both families is the same byte-identical sprue → the same trampoline:

```
tabtarget (3-line sprue)
  → exec z-launcher.sh  <basename> <args>     # SAME trampoline for all
  → trampoline reads BURD_LAUNCHER, resolves it under rbml_launchers/
  → launcher.<id>_workbench.sh → bul_launch → workbench
```

Up to "read `BURD_LAUNCHER`" they are mechanically identical. Then they fork.

## Where they diverge

**1. Different `BURD_LAUNCHER` → different workbench**

| Family | `BURD_LAUNCHER` | Workbench |
|--------|----------------|-----------|
| `rbtd-*` | `launcher.rbtw_workbench.sh` | `rbtw_workbench.sh` (theurge) |
| `rbw-t*` | `launcher.rbw_workbench.sh` | `rbw_workbench.sh` (Recipe Bottle) |

These are two entirely separate workbenches in two orthogonal pipelines (RBTW vs RBW — the CLAUDE.md "two orthogonal Rust pipelines" note).

**2. Different in-workbench dispatch mechanism**

- **`rbtd-*` → hand-written `case` statement.** `rbtw_workbench.sh` calls `rbte_dispatch` (`rbte_cli.sh:46`), a literal `case "${z_command}"` switch on the colophon (`rbtd-b`/`rbtd-t`/`rbtd-r`/`rbtd-s`), with a nested `case` on the frontispiece token for `rbtd-s`. No registry.

- **`rbw-t*` → table-driven zipper lookup.** `rbw_workbench.sh` calls `buz_exec_lookup "${z_command}"` (`rbw_workbench.sh:69`), which resolves the colophon against the **zipper registry** — the `buz_enroll RBZ_QUALIFY_* "rbw-tf"/"rbw-tr"/… "rbq_qualify_*"` entries in `rbz_zipper.sh:200-204`. The colophon → function mapping is data, not a switch.

**3. `rbw_workbench` carries extra machinery the theurge path lacks**

`rbw_route` runs `zrbz_healthcheck` and a **qualification gate** (`rbq_qualify_fast` before `rbw-cC`/`rbw-fO`) — `rbtw_workbench` is a "pure router: no inline logic, no fixture knowledge, no kindle chains" (its own header comment) and does none of that.

## Verdict

No — `rbtd-*` and `rbw-t*` are **not** routed the same way past the shared trampoline. They share the BUK sprue+`BURD_LAUNCHER`+`z-launcher.sh` convention (that's universal to every tabtarget in the repo), but they land in different workbenches and use fundamentally different dispatch strategies: theurge uses a hardcoded `case` switch, while RBW uses the zipper-registry `buz_exec_lookup` table.

This is also *why* the `rbq_Qualify.sh:182` bug is possible: a `rbw-t*` qualify function (RBW pipeline) reaches across the boundary to invoke a `rbtd-s` suite tabtarget (theurge pipeline) by hardcoded path string — and that string went stale. The two pipelines compose only through these explicit cross-calls, not through shared routing.

content.content:
# buz_exec_lookup() - Resolve colophon via registry and exec
# Args: colophon, base_dir [, extra args passed through to exec]
# Execs: BUZ_FOLIO=<folio> ${base_dir}/${module} ${command} [extra args]
# Dies if colophon not found
buz_exec_lookup() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local z_colophon="${1:-}"
  local z_base_dir="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_colophon}" || buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: colophon required"
  test -n "${z_base_dir}" || buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: base_dir required"
  shift 2

  # Find colophon in registry
  local z_found=""
  local z_i=""
  for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
    test "${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}" = "${z_colophon}" || continue
    z_found="${z_i}"
    break
  done
  test -n "${z_found}" || buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: colophon not found: ${z_colophon}"

  # Decode folio from channel
  local z_folio=""
  local z_args=("$@")
  case "${z_buz_channel_roll[z_found]}" in
    "")
      if (( ${#z_args[@]} )); then

    *)
      buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: unknown channel: ${z_buz_channel_roll[z_found]}"
      ;;
  esac

  # Dispatch with folio in exec environment
  BUZ_FOLIO="${z_folio}" exec "${z_base_dir}/${z_buz_module_roll[z_found]}" "${z_buz_command_roll[z_found]}" ${z_args[@]+"${z_args[@]}"}
}

# buz_exec_lookup() - Resolve colophon via registry and exec
# Args: colophon, base_dir [, extra args passed through to exec]
# Execs: BUZ_FOLIO=<folio> ${base_dir}/${module} ${command} [extra args]
# Dies if colophon not found
buz_exec_lookup() {
  zbuz_sentinel

  local z_colophon="${1:-}"
  local z_base_dir="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_colophon}" || buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: colophon required"
  test -n "${z_base_dir}" || buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: base_dir required"
  shift 2

  # Find colophon in registry
  local z_found=""
  local z_i=""
  for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
    test "${z_buz_colophon_roll[z_i]}" = "${z_colophon}" || continue
    z_found="${z_i}"
    break
  done
  test -n "${z_found}" || buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: colophon not found: ${z_colophon}"

  # Decode folio from channel
  local z_folio=""
  local z_args=("$@")
  case "${z_buz_channel_roll[z_found]}" in
    "")
      if (( ${#z_args[@]} )); then
        buc_warn "Colophon '${z_colophon}' takes no folio; ignoring unexpected argument(s): ${z_args[*]}"
        z_args=()
      fi
      ;;
    "imprint")
      z_folio="${BURD_TOKEN_3}"
      ;;
    "param1")
      if (( ${#z_args[@]} )); then
        z_folio="${z_args[0]}"
        z_args=("${z_args[@]:1}")
      fi
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "buz_exec_lookup: unknown channel: ${z_buz_channel_roll[z_found]}"
      ;;
  esac

  # Dispatch with folio in exec environment
  BUZ_FOLIO="${z_folio}" exec "${z_base_dir}/${z_buz_module_roll[z_found]}" "${z_buz_command_roll[z_found]}" ${z_args[@]+"${z_args[@]}"}
}

# eof

content.text:
Now I have the complete picture, traced to the bottom of both stacks. Here's the full process trace.

## Full bash-process trace, both families

**`rbtd-*` (direct path):**
```
sprue → z-launcher.sh → launcher.rbtw_workbench.sh → rbtw_workbench.sh
  → rbte_dispatch (case switch, rbte_cli.sh:46)
    → rbte_build / rbte_test / rbte_run / rbte_suite / rbte_single
      → cargo build  +  exec target/debug/rbtd <manifest> <fixture>
```

**`rbw-t*` (qualify path):**
```
sprue → z-launcher.sh → launcher.rbw_workbench.sh → rbw_workbench.sh
  → buz_exec_lookup (registry)  → rbq_cli.sh rbq_qualify_*
    ├─ rbq_qualify_fast      → static lint only — NEVER touches theurge
    ├─ rbq_qualify_release   → fast + shellcheck + exec tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh  ⚠ BROKEN PATH
    ├─ rbq_qualify_pristine  → exec tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh   ─┐
    ├─ rbq_qualify_skirmish  → exec tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.skirmish.sh   ─┤ re-enters the
    └─ rbq_qualify_tadmor    → exec tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.{kludge-tadmor,tadmor}.sh ─┘ rbtd-* path
                                          as fresh child processes (from the top)
```

## Answer

**At the very bottom, yes — every fixture that actually runs is executed by the same theurge command: the compiled Rust binary `target/debug/rbtd`, invoked once per fixture with `<colophon-manifest> <fixture>`** (`rbte_engine.sh:157/175/189`). All of `rbte_run`, `rbte_suite`, and `rbte_single` funnel into that single executable. So the *workload* converges.

**But they are not "managed by the same theurge commands" in the dispatch sense.** The convergence happens by **child-process re-entry**, not shared routing:

- The `rbw-t*` qualify functions never call `rbte_dispatch`. When they need fixtures, they **shell out to a fresh `rbtd-*` tabtarget** (`"${PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"`), which spins up a *new* process that re-enters the theurge stack from the very top (sprue → z-launcher → rbtw_workbench → rbte_dispatch → binary). Two separate bash process trees that touch only through this `exec` of a path string.

- The coupling is by **hardcoded tabtarget path**, not a function call or shared module. That's exactly the fragility that produced the `rbw-ts` → `rbtd-s` rot: `rbq_qualify_release` points at a path that no longer exists, so the one qualify level that's *supposed* to re-enter the theurge binary will instead die before reaching it.

- Two of the five never reach theurge by these means: **`rbq_qualify_fast`** runs pure static checks (no binary), and **`rbq_qualify_release`** is currently broken before its re-entry.

So: same final executor (the `rbtd` binary), reached by **two different dispatch mechanisms** (in-process `case` for `rbtd-*`; cross-pipeline child-process `exec` of a path string for `rbw-tP/tS/tT`), with one of those cross-pipeline links (`rbw-tr`) currently severed.

The `rbq_Qualify.sh:182` fix (`rbw-ts` → `rbtd-s`) is the one concrete repair this whole trace surfaces. Want me to make it?

content.text:
I've now traced the gate and the dispatch model deep enough to answer concretely. Here's what I found, and it changes the picture in your favor on one point but raises three real losses.

## Good news first: the gate is benign

`zrbz_healthcheck` → `buz_healthcheck` (`buz_zipper.sh:219`) is **not** a regime-state check. It only verifies that every *enrolled* colophon has a tabtarget file on disk. So routing suites through `rbw_workbench` would **not** impose any regime-health precondition — the gauntlet/pristine suite could still run from a blank marshal-zero state. The "you'd couple test-running to RBW regime health" fear is unfounded.

(Side note this surfaced: `buz_healthcheck` only checks *enrolled* colophons. The broken `rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh` is a hardcoded path string inside `rbq_qualify_release`, not an enrollment — which is exactly why the healthcheck never catches it. It also confirms suites *used* to live under `rbw-ts`; that path is a fossil of the arrangement you're asking to restore.)

## What you actually lose

**1. `FixtureCase` does not fit the imprint model — this is the real blocker.**

`rbtd-s.FixtureCase.sh` (→ `rbte_single`) is variadic with list-by-omission semantics (per CLAUDE.md): zero args → list all fixtures; one arg → list cases for that fixture; two args → run one case against an already-charged crucible. This is the crucible-debug loop.

The imprint channel bakes a single folio into the *filename* (one tabtarget per target). It cannot express "any fixture × any case, or omit to list." The zipper's only param-bearing channel is `param1` (single positional), and its "no folio" path collides with the "omit to list" semantics. You could rebuild it behind a `param1` colophon with a wrapper, but it is **not** convertible to an imprint — so a pure "convert to imprint" plan drops the single-case debugger, which is the most-used theurge tool during crucible work.

**2. Four of the six suites have no `rbw-t*` home today.**

Current `rbw-t*` only covers `gauntlet` (via `tP`) and `skirmish` (via `tS`). `fast`/`service`/`crucible`/`complete` have **no** standalone `rbw-t` entry — `complete` is only reachable buried inside `rbw-tr` *after* lint+shellcheck. So unless you mint `rbw-t?.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh` imprints, deleting `rbtd-s` removes the only way to run those four suites by name. (Preservable — but it's net-new enrollment work, not a deletion.)

**3. Family fracture + orthogonality regression.**

Your proposal deletes only `rbtd-s`, but `rbtd-b` (build), `rbtd-t` (test), and the 17 `rbtd-r.FixtureRun.*` (single-fixture) **all remain** in the theurge pipeline. So:
- You don't get to delete `rbtw_workbench`, its launcher, or `rbte_cli`/`rbte_engine` — they stay for `rbtd-b/t/r`.
- You'd split "run one fixture" (`rbtd-r`, theurge pipeline) from "run a suite of fixtures" (`rbw-t`, RBW pipeline) — same binary, two workbenches, by family. That's *less* coherent than today.
- `rbtw_workbench`'s explicit design is "pure router: no inline logic, no kindle chains, orthogonal from VOW, permanently part of rbk." Suites under `rbw-t` would run through the full RBW kindle chain (`zbuv`/`zburd`/`zbuz`/`zrbz`) instead. CLAUDE.md's "two orthogonal Rust pipelines" principle is the thing being traded away.

## My read

You don't lose any *test-running* capability that can't be rebuilt — except `FixtureCase`, which genuinely doesn't map to imprints. But the change doesn't *consolidate*; it fractures the `rbtd-{b,t,r,s}` family and folds test dispatch back into the RBW pipeline that was deliberately split out (the `rbw-ts` fossil is evidence this split was a prior, intentional move).

There's a cleaner way to get the ergonomics you want without any of this. Which of these are you actually after?

.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json:1000:              "jjgtn_text": "## Character
Vocabulary mint plus single-file exemplar; the exemplar validates the vocabulary on first use.

## Goal
Add `== Control Voicings` to BUS0 declaring `busc_*` (bash console) and `bush_*` (handbook/operator) quoin families. Refactor BUSJIW as the worked exemplar.

## Boundary
- Vocabulary modeled on RBS0 §Control Voicings (`rbbc_*`, `rbhg_*`); each verb is its own quoin with anchor + attribute reference + short semantic.
- BUSJIW refactor: sub-step grammar with control verbs, `«VAR»` names for cross-state, bounded NOTEs only, decomposed guarantee.
- Other BUSJ* spec files out of scope — that is pace 2.
- No bash code edits.

## Done when
- BUS0 mapping section + Control Voicings section land both families.
- BUSJIW reads as a sibling of RBSAC/RBSAV in style.
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` and `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green.",

.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json:1244:              "jjgtn_text": "## Character

Implementation. Single pace, layered scope: Regime → Module → Tabtargets
→ Spec/Integration → Verification.

## Docket

Land the design from heat paddock and BUS0 spec (post-AAp split:
fenestrate + garrison sibling verbs sharing one implementation module).

### L1. Regime & Constants

- Implement four-field BURP, drop `BURN_WORKLOADS`, add
  `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` per paddock.
- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` enrolls as xname (1–32), format-checked by buv.
  No collision list, no allowed-set enumeration — it is just the SSH
  login name used to reach a node as the workload operator.
- `bub` / `bube` / `bubep_*` tree minted; BUBC tinder constants placed
  (`BUBC_platforms_{windows,linux,mac}` holding `bubep_*`); `BURN_PLATFORM`
  re-enrolled with `bubep_linux bubep_mac bubep_windows`.

### L2. Module & Helper

- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh` BCG-compliant module per paddock.
  Sub-letter `b` = bash; legend added to BUS0 mapping section per Quoin
  Sub-Letter Discipline.
- `bujb_resolve_investiture` is sole load-and-cross-validate entrypoint,
  exposes `BUJB_RESOLVED_*` globals (set listed in paddock), single-call-
  per-process, readonly after resolution.
- Helper enforces at resolve-time: `BURP_VICEROYALTY` registered in BURN;
  key files exist with mode `0600`; privkey loads via `ssh-keygen -y` dry-
  load (proves parseable + unencrypted; replaces vague "key-pair self-
  consistency" wording — there is no second artifact, only the privkey);
  WSL default distribution = `rbtww-main`.

### L3. Tabtargets & Ceremonies

- 14 tabtargets per paddock catalog: 11 net-new + 3 modify-in-place
  (`buw-rn[lrv]` already exist, reshape for new BURN four-field shape;
  `buw-rp[lrv]` are net-new alongside `buw-jpF`, `buw-jpG[bcw]`,
  `buw-jwk`, `buw-jwc`, `buw-jws`, `buw-hj0`).
- Operational tabtargets call `bujb_resolve_investiture` first, then
  assert per-verb platform invariant inline (2-line check; helper stays
  generic): fenestrate = `bubep_windows`; garrison-b ∈ {`bubep_linux`,
  `bubep_mac`}; garrison-c/w = `bubep_windows`.
- Fenestrate ceremony per BUSJPF two-phase contract; bash orchestrator
  treats post-restart disconnect as expected phase boundary.
- Garrison ceremony per BUSJG[BCW] (workload-only, 6 steps); first admin-
  SSH failure surfaces a clear pointer to fenestrate handbook (Windows)
  or operator-managed `ssh-copy-id` (Linux/Mac).

### L4. Spec & Integration

- BUS0 / BUSJGB / BUSJGC / BUSJGW specify the three concrete `command=`
  directive strings (b/c/w) as locked spec content, sourced by the
  module rather than invented there. `command=` is a documented OpenSSH
  `authorized_keys(5)` directive; standard usage.
- BUS0 drift sweep folded in: `:burp_node:` linked term at line 127 →
  `:burp_viceroyalty:` (and any sibling stale `BURP_NODE` / `BURP_USER` /
  `BURP_SSH_PUBKEY` / `BURP_KEY_FILE` / `BURP_COMMAND` references AAp's
  sweep missed). Verify with `grep -n` before completion.
- Superseded colophons retired (audit `buw-np*`, `buw-nw*`, old
  `buw-HW*`); AAm prunes only those directly replaced by the new 14-
  target catalog. Broad sweep deferred to AAn.
- RBK Windows orchestrator (`rbhw_windows.sh`, `rbw-HWd[cdw]`,
  `rbw-h0`, `rbw-hw`) rewired to fenestrate + garrison + handbook.

### L5. Verification

- `bash -n` green on all edited shell files.
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` unchanged from baseline.

### Out of scope

PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred); adopt verb (deferred); user
regime (deferred); Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb (deferred — fenestrate
is Windows-OpenSSH only); shell-letter availability discovery on BURN
side; end-to-end operator test on a real Windows node (lives in
AAD/E/F); broad cleanup sweep (lives in AAn); operator data migration
(no migration — pre-release, nothing working to preserve).",

  treats post-restart disconnect as expected phase boundary.

- Superseded colophons retired (audit `buw-np*`, `buw-nw*`, old
  `buw-HW*`); AAm prunes only those directly replaced by the new 14-
  target catalog. Broad sweep deferred to AAn.
- RBK Windows orchestrator (`rbhw_windows.sh`, `rbw-HWd[cdw]`,
  `rbw-h0`, `rbw-hw`) rewired to fenestrate + garrison + handbook.

### L5. Verification

- `bash -n` green on all edited shell files.
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` unchanged from baseline.

### Out of scope

PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred); adopt verb (deferred); user
regime (deferred); Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb (deferred — fenestrate
is Windows-OpenSSH only); shell-letter availability discovery on BURN
side; end-to-end operator test on a real Windows node (lives in
AAD/E/F); broad cleanup sweep (lives in AAn); operator data migration
(no migration — pre-release, nothing working to preserve).",
.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json:1258:              "jjgtn_text": "## Character

Implementation. Single pace, layered scope: Regime → Module → Tabtargets
→ Integration → Verification.

## Docket

Land the design from the heat paddock and BUS0 spec (post-AAp split:
fenestrate + garrison are sibling verbs sharing one implementation
module).

### L1. Regime & Constants

- `BURN_WORKLOADS` dropped.
- BURP restructured to four fields: `BURP_VICEROYALTY` (BURN reference),
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Privileged/workload asymmetric: USER is
  privileged-only because the workload user is project-wide convention
  (lives in BURC); KEY_FILE has both variants because each operator owns
  their own key material.
- BURP drops: `BURP_SSH_PUBKEY` (derive via `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time),
  `BURP_USER` (split), `BURP_KEY_FILE` (split), `BURP_NODE` (renamed to
  `BURP_VICEROYALTY` to match BURN's identifier vocabulary), `BURP_COMMAND`
  (moves to module per-shell-letter hardcoded mapping).
- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` added with name-collision blacklist + length
  validation.
- Encrypted privkeys rejected fast (would hang `ssh-keygen -y`).
- Prefix minted: `bub` = Bootstrap (parent non-terminal); `bube` = enum
  sub-namespace; `bubep_*` = `BURN_PLATFORM` enum values. Future enums
  follow this precedent.
- BUBC tinder constants (BCG lower_snake form):
  `BUBC_platforms_windows`, `BUBC_platforms_linux`,
  `BUBC_platforms_mac`, holding `bubep_windows`, `bubep_linux`,
  `bubep_mac`.
- `BURN_PLATFORM` enum re-enrolled with `bubep_linux bubep_mac
  bubep_windows`.

### L2. Module & Junction Helper

- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh` BCG-compliant module created.
- Module hardcodes: three shell-letter → `command=` directive mappings
  (b/c/w); workload privkey destination paths on remote per
  shell-letter; WSL distribution name `rbtww-main`; Windows OpenSSH
  `sshd_config` hardening directive set used by fenestrate.
- `bujb_resolve_investiture <investiture>` is the sole load-and-cross-
  validate entrypoint used by every operational tabtarget. Exposes
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_*` globals (readonly after resolution; single-call-
  per-process): `BUJB_RESOLVED_INVESTITURE`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_VICEROYALTY`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_HOST`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_PLATFORM`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_PRIVILEGED_USER`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_USER`
  (from BURC), `BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`.
- Helper enforces: `BURP_VICEROYALTY` resolves to a registered BURN
  viceroyalty; key files exist + correct perms + key-pair self-
  consistency; privkeys not encrypted.
- WSL hardcodes `rbtww-main`; mismatched current default fails fast.

### L3. Tabtargets & Ceremonies

- 14 tabtargets per paddock catalog, zipper-enrolled:
  - 6 regime config `buw-r[np][lrv]`: `buw-rn[lrv]` already exist
    (modify-in-place to reflect new BURN shape); `buw-rp[lrv]` are new.
  - `buw-jpF` (fenestrate), `buw-jpG[bcw]` (3 garrison),
    `buw-jwk`, `buw-jwc`, `buw-jws`, `buw-hj0`.
- Operational tabtargets call `bujb_resolve_investiture` first, then
  add per-verb invariants:
  - Fenestrate: platform = `bubep_windows`.
  - Garrison-b: platform ∈ {`bubep_linux`, `bubep_mac`}.
  - Garrison-c/w: platform = `bubep_windows`.
- Fenestrate ceremony works per BUSJPF two-phase contract; bash
  orchestrator treats post-restart disconnect as expected phase
  boundary.
- Garrison ceremony works per BUSJGB/C/W (workload-only, 6 steps);
  first admin-SSH failure surfaces a clear pointer to the bootstrap
  handbook procedure.

### L4. Integration

- Superseded colophons retired or reshaped (audit `buw-np*`, `buw-nw*`,
  old `buw-HW*` per cleanup-prior-attempts; AAm prunes only those
  directly replaced by the new 14-target catalog; broad sweep deferred
  to AAn).
- RBK Windows orchestrator entries (`rbhw_windows.sh`, `rbw-HWd[cdw]`,
  `rbw-h0`, `rbw-hw`) rewired to fenestrate + garrison + handbook.

### L5. Verification

- `bash -n` green on all edited shell files.
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` unchanged from baseline.

### Out of scope

PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred); adopt verb (deferred); user
regime (deferred); Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb (deferred —
fenestrate is Windows-OpenSSH only); shell-letter availability
discovery on BURN side; end-to-end operator test on a real Windows
node (lives in AAD/E/F); broad cleanup sweep (lives in AAn).",

- Fenestrate ceremony works per BUSJPF two-phase contract; bash

  `BUJB_RESOLVED_VICEROYALTY`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_HOST`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_PLATFORM`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_PRIVILEGED_USER`,
  `BUJB_RESOLVED_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`, `BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_USER`
  (from BURC), `BUJB_RESOLVED_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`.
- Helper enforces: BURP_NODE resolves to a registered BURN viceroyalty;
  key files exist + correct perms + key-pair self-consistency; privkeys
  not encrypted.
- WSL hardcodes `rbtww-main`; mismatched current default fails fast.

### L3. Tabtargets & Ceremonies

- 14 tabtargets per paddock catalog, zipper-enrolled:
  `buw-r[np][lrv]` (6 regime config), `buw-jpF` (fenestrate),
  `buw-jpG[bcw]` (3 garrison), `buw-jwk`, `buw-jwc`, `buw-jws`,
  `buw-hj0`.
- Operational tabtargets call `bujb_resolve_investiture` first, then
  add per-verb invariants:
  - Fenestrate: platform = `bubep_windows`.
  - Garrison-b: platform ∈ {`bubep_linux`, `bubep_mac`}.
  - Garrison-c/w: platform = `bubep_windows`.
- Fenestrate ceremony works per BUSJPF two-phase contract; bash
  orchestrator treats post-restart disconnect as expected phase
  boundary.
- Garrison ceremony works per BUSJGB/C/W (workload-only, 6 steps);
  first admin-SSH failure surfaces a clear pointer to the bootstrap
  handbook procedure.

### L4. Integration

- Superseded colophons (those directly replaced by the new 14-target
  catalog) retired or reshaped; broad sweep deferred to AAn.
- RBK Windows orchestrator entries (`rbhw_windows.sh`, `rbw-HWd[cdw]`,
  `rbw-h0`, `rbw-hw`) rewired to fenestrate + garrison + handbook.

### L5. Verification

- `bash -n` green on all edited shell files.
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` unchanged from baseline.

### Out of scope

PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred); adopt verb (deferred); user
regime (deferred); Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb (deferred —
fenestrate is Windows-OpenSSH only); shell-letter availability
discovery on BURN side; end-to-end operator test on a real Windows
node (lives in AAD/E/F); broad cleanup sweep (lives in AAn).",
.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json:1279:              "jjgtn_text": "## Character

Implementation — drop BURW, reshape BURP, mint module, build tabtargets,
fenestrate + garrison ceremonies. Single seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh`)
houses both verbs.

## Docket

Land the design from the heat paddock and BUS0 spec (post-AAp split:
fenestrate + garrison are sibling verbs sharing one implementation
module).

Done when:

- BURW infrastructure removed (`burw_regime.sh`, `burw_cli.sh`, `burw.env`
  template, any BURW tabtargets, zipper enrollments, validators)
- BURP regime restructured to four-field shape (`BURP_NODE`,
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`); pubkey derivation via `ssh-keygen -y`;
  validation surface updated (file existence, perms, key-pair match);
  encrypted privkeys rejected fast (would hang `ssh-keygen -y`)
- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` field added with name-collision blacklist + length
  validation
- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh` BCG-compliant module created;
  houses both fenestrate and garrison; hardcodes the three
  shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w) used by garrison,
  the workload privkey destination paths on remote per shell-letter, the
  canonical WSL distribution name (`rbtww-main`), and the Windows
  OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive set used by fenestrate
- All 14 tabtargets per paddock catalog implemented and zipper-enrolled
  (6 BURN/BURP regime config; 1 fenestrate `buw-jpF`; 3 garrison
  `buw-jpG[bcw]`; knock `buw-jwk`; command-file `buw-jwc`; interactive
  `buw-jws`; handbook `buw-hj0`)
- Fenestrate ceremony works end-to-end on Windows OpenSSH per BUSJPF
  two-phase contract:
  * phase 1 over `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` admin
    SSH (steady-state authenticates by key; first-run prompts on
    /dev/tty, operator types Windows admin password once, fenestrate
    process never sees it) → idempotently place admin pubkey (bare; no
    `command=` since fenestrate is uniform) in
    `administrators_authorized_keys` → `icacls` lockdown → write
    hardened directives (`PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
    `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`) → verify via
    PowerShell `Get-Content` (raw bytes) + bash-side BCG-compliant
    parse → `sshd -t` (penultimate atomic op) → `Restart-Service sshd`
    (final atomic op of phase 1)
  * phase 2 reconnects with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` only
    (no password fallback — failure here surfaces a brick) and verifies
    post-state
  * bash orchestrator treats the post-restart disconnect as the expected
    phase boundary, not a failure
- Garrison ceremony works end-to-end on b, c, and w shell variants per
  revised BUSJGB/C/W (workload-only, 6 steps): admin SSH (key auth only;
  no password path) → destroy workload user (userdel -r or platform
  equivalent) → create fresh workload user named `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`
  (unprivileged) → place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys
  with shell-letter `command=` directive → copy workload privkey to
  remote workload account at the per-shell-letter hardcoded path →
  validate round-trip (SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0)
- Garrison's first action (admin SSH via key auth) fails fast with a
  pointer to the bootstrap handbook procedure when admin trust is not
  yet in place
- WSL invocation hardcodes `rbtww-main`; mismatched current default
  WSL distribution fails fast with a clear error
- Superseded colophons retired or reshaped (audit `buw-np*` / `buw-nw*`
  / old `buw-HW*` per cleanup-prior-attempts AAn scope; AAm itself
  prunes only those directly replaced by the new 14-tabtarget catalog)
- RBK Windows orchestrator entries (`rbhw_windows.sh`, `rbw-HWd[cdw]`,
  `rbw-h0`, `rbw-hw`) updated to point at the new fenestrate + garrison
  + handbook
- `bash -n` green on all edited shell files; `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh`
  green; `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` unchanged from baseline

Out of scope: PowerShell garrison + dispatch (deferred per paddock);
adopt verb (deferred); user regime (deferred); Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening
verb (deferred — fenestrate handles Windows OpenSSH only); end-to-end
operator test on a real Windows node (validation belongs to AAD/E/F
practice paces); broad cleanup sweep (lives in AAn).",

.claude/jjm/retired/jjh_b260402-r260409-rbk-mvp-3-onboarding-guides.md:3599:Retire rbgm_ManualProcedures.sh; split into rbho_onboarding.sh (handbook cross-role walkthroughs, thin deps) and rbhp_payor.sh (handbook payor-only ceremonies, full GCP stack). Delete rbgm_onboarding() retired stub, rbgm_LEGACY_setup_admin() ITCH marker, and zrbgm_po_review_defaults (truly unused). Migrate 6 walkthrough functions + probe helpers (po_status, po_extract_capture, probe_role_credentials, probe_retriever/director/governor/payor_units, triage_role) to zrbho_*; migrate 3 payor ceremonies (rbhp_establish, rbhp_refresh, rbhp_quota_build — quota rename deferred out of scope per docket) + full zrbhp_* display combinators and ZRBHP_* color state. Slim rbhp_kindle — drop unused temp-file constants that were for retired LEGACY_setup_admin and ONBOARDING_MONIKER/_NAMEPLATE that were only referenced from retired rbgm_onboarding dead code. Split rbgm_cli.sh into rbho_cli.sh (thin: buh/buz/rbcc/rbgc/rbz) and rbhp_cli.sh (full regime+OAuth+IAM stack). Update rbz_zipper.sh colophon routing: rbw-go/rbw-gO* -> rbho_cli.sh, rbw-gPE/rbw-gPR/rbw-gq -> rbhp_cli.sh (rbw-gPI remains in rbgp_cli.sh as docket predicted). Update CLAUDE.md acronym map (remove RBGM, add RBHO + RBHP with rbh*=Handbook family note and reserved RBHG/RBHD/RBHR slots). Update RBS0-SpecTop.adoc 3 display-text refs to point at new function names. Docket deviation: the probe_*_units and po_* helpers were listed in docket as 'used only by old monolithic' but are actually called directly by per-role walkthroughs — they were migrated to rbho as zrbho_* rather than deleted. Validation: tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 75/75, tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh passes (tabtarget structure, colophon registrations, generated context), manual smoke rbw-go/gOR/gOD/gOG/gOP/gOr/gPE/gPR/gq all render with OSC-8 hyperlinks and resolved tabtarget colophons. Size limit raised to 150000 — rbho_onboarding.sh (~42KB) plus rbhp_payor.sh (~21KB) plus two new cli files plus full rbgm_ManualProcedures.sh deletion (1867 lines) make this unsplittable: partial commits would leave zipper routing broken or create dangling symbol references. Unblocks later A3 paces that reshape role tracks inside rbho_onboarding.sh.

.claude/jjm/retired/jjh_b260415-r260514-rbk-10-mvp-regen-retest-depot.md:2793:`rbtdrc_four_mode_supply_chain` is currently one `rbtdre_Case` with a 15-step sequential pipeline and a single pass/fail verdict. The engine's `rbtdre_Section` already supports multi-case sections (`cases: &[…]` is plural) — four-mode just doesn't use it. Decompose so single-case reruns via `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.four-mode.sh <name>` actually target meaningful sub-units, failures attribute precisely ("bind failed" vs. the current "four-mode step 2"), and future fixture additions land as new cases rather than step insertions into an ever-lengthening monolith.

content.text:
I have the full blast radius now. Here's my assessment of your proposal — and it's better than you framed it, because the consolidation reaches further than `rbtd-s`.

## Verdict: your proposal loses zero tests, and the single-pipeline merge is clean

The key feasibility facts from the trace:

- **`zrbz_healthcheck` is just a zipper-integrity check** (every enrolled colophon has a tabtarget on disk) — no regime-state coupling. So hosting suites under `rbw-t*` imposes no new precondition. The gauntlet still runs from marshal-zero.
- **`rbte_engine.sh` is the real logic** (suite arrays, binary build, fixture exec). It's sourced by a workbench; it doesn't care *which* one. So "one pipeline" = have `rbw_workbench` reach `rbte_*` via the zipper registry (`buz_exec_lookup` execs `${module} ${command}`), and **delete `rbtw_workbench.sh` + `launcher.rbtw_workbench.sh` + the `rbtd-*` case switch in `rbte_cli.sh`**. The Rust binary and `rbte_engine.sh` survive unchanged.
- **Imprint colophons with many tabtargets already work** — `rbw-cC.Charge.{tadmor,srjcl,pluml}.sh` is the existing precedent. So `rbw-ts.TestSuite.<imprint>` is proven-feasible.

## The consolidation map (more collapses than just rbtd-s)

| Today | Proposed | Count change |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| `rbtd-b.Build` / `rbtd-t.Test` | `rbw-tb.Build` / `rbw-tt.Test` | 2 → 2 |
| `rbtd-s.TestSuite.{6 imprints}` | `rbw-ts.TestSuite.{imprints}` | 6 → 6–7 |
| `rbtd-s.FixtureCase` (variadic) | `rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh <fixture> [case]` (param) | 1 → 1 |
| `rbtd-r.FixtureRun.{17}` | `rbw-t?.FixtureRun.sh <fixture>` (param) **or** keep 17 imprints | 17 → 1 *(or 17)* |
| `rbw-tP` (pure exec gauntlet) | **fold into** `rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh` | −1 |
| `rbw-tS` (pure exec skirmish) | **fold into** `rbw-ts.TestSuite.skirmish.sh` | −1 |
| `rbw-tT` (exec kludge-tadmor + tadmor) | **fold into** new `tadmor` suite imprint | −1 |
| `rbw-tf` QualifyFast (lint, no fixtures) | **keep** — genuinely non-suite | 0 |
| `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease (lint+shellcheck+complete) | **keep**, fix its internal call (kills the `rbw-ts` rot by construction) | 0 |
| `rbw-tK`/`rbw-tO` (kludge/ordain cycle) | **keep** — separate build+install concern | 0 |

`rbw-tP/tS/tT` are **pure one-line suite execs** (confirmed in `rbq_Qualify.sh:189-244`) — they're redundant the moment `rbw-ts.<imprint>` exists. Folding them is the big win. Net: **~30 test/qualify tabtargets → ~13**, plus deleting `rbtw_workbench.sh`, its launcher, and the `rbte_dispatch` switch.

## Blast radius is small and entirely in-repo

Live callers that must change (everything else is `.claude/jjm/` history — archival, leave alone):

- **Code:** `rbq_Qualify.sh` (4 path strings + the `:182` rot), `rbte_cli.sh` (restructure dispatch), two error-message strings in `rbte_engine.sh`, two help-text strings in the Rust binary (`main.rs`, `rbtdth_helpers.rs`). *(The `rbtd-test-*` scratch-dir names in `rbtdte_engine.rs`/`rbtdti_invocation.rs` are not tabtarget refs — leave them.)*
- **Operator-facing handbook displays:** `rbhoda_director_airgap.sh:293`, `rbhots_tadmor_security.sh:168,177` (`buh_tt` calls that *show operators the command* — must show the new names).
- **Docs:** `CLAUDE.md`, `claude-buk-core.md:95`, `rbk-claude-acronyms.md:32` (RBTW mapping), `rbk-claude-theurge-ifrit-context.md`, `CLAUDE.consumer.md:119-121`.

**No `.github`/CI references. No fundus/cerebro coupling** (the JJK fundus tests dispatch `jjw-*`, not `rbtd-*`). So this migrates atomically with no external breakage.

**One thing to weigh:** `CLAUDE.consumer.md` ships to consumer projects via `rbk-prep-release` — so `rbtd-s`/`rbtd-r` → `rbw-t*` is a **consumer-visible colophon rename**, release-note-worthy, not purely internal.

Three decisions genuinely fork the design — the rest I can mint per discipline (`ts`/`tc`/`tb`/`tt` are free; I'd avoid `tF`/`tR` since they case-collide with `tf`/`tr` per the case-sensitivity rule):

## Paces

### cloud-depot-build-viability-fixture (₢BMAAH) [rough]

## Character
Test-infrastructure fixture; mechanical once shape is set. A cheap cloud-build
probe — confirms the build/retrieve path yields a runnable artifact, with no
crucible apparatus.

## Goal
Prove the cloud-depot build-and-retrieve path yields a *runnable* artifact:
ordain (conjure-mode) `rbev-busybox` → summon → bare container-runtime run of a
degenerate command (assert exit 0) → abjure. Standing depot, no per-run levy.

## Suite placement — sibling to skirmish, NOT a member of it
This is a new standing-depot scenario-chain suite named `dogfight`, sibling to
`skirmish` in the operator-precondition family (both reuse a standing
operator-levied depot via the `canonical-invest` idiom — no levy, no unmake).
The two differ on the crucible axis and that difference is the whole point:
`skirmish` charges the four crucibles to prove containment; `dogfight` charges

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BM
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
6	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
7	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
8	`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
9	across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
10	fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
11	enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
12	work, prone to silent drift.
13	
14	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
15	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
16	uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
17	the sweep.
18	
19	The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
20	messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
21	durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
22	discovery recipe, not here.
23	
24	## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay
25	
26	"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
27	literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
28	character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
29	API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
30	textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
31	`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
32	build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.
33	
34	MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
35	an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
36	the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
37	prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
38	(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
39	inventing rationale.
40	
41	Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
42	dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
43	local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
44	(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
45	one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
46	text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
47	concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.
48	
49	## Convention — locked
50	
51	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
52	
53	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
54	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
55	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
56	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
57	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
58	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
59	             `e` = environment, etc.)
60	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
61	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
62	
63	Full target table (all in-scope enums):
64	
65	| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
66	|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
67	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
68	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
69	| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
70	| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
71	| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
72	| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |
73	
74	Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
75	egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
76	share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
77	DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
78	variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
79	whole cluster.
80	
81	## Scope decisions — resolved
82	
83	- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
84	- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
85	  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
86	  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
87	- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
88	  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
89	  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
90	- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
91	  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
92	  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
93	  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
94	  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
95	  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
96	  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
97	  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
98	- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
99	  unchanged from original framing).
100	
101	## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work
102	
103	Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
104	the candidate list and classify each hit:
105	
106	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
107	   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
108	2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
109	   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
110	   **Migrate.**
111	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
112	   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
113	   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
114	   alone.**
115	4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
116	   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
117	   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
118	   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
119	   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
120	     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
121	   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
122	   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
123	   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
124	   `-tether`.
125	
126	### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses
127	
128	- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
129	  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
130	  `:-bind`, etc.
131	- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
132	  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
133	  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
134	  stale.
135	- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
136	  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
137	  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
138	  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
139	  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).
140	
141	### Noise ranking (classification effort)
142	
143	Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
144	prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
145	`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
146	class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
147	is the heavy classification grind.
148	
149	## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate
150	
151	Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
152	(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
153	context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
154	literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
155	declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
156	remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
157	until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.
158	
159	**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
160	sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
161	graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
162	suites.
163	
164	## Discovery recipe
165	
166	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
167	  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
168	  enum missed.
169	- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
170	  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
171	- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
172	  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
173	  tables.
174	- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
175	  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
176	  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.
177	
178	## Posture
179	
180	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
181	execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
182	pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.
183	
184	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
185	`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
186	gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
187	remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
188	above.
189	
190	## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)
191	
192	₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
193	(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
194	not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
195	concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
196	wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
197	is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
198	intact.
199	
200	What remains and matters:
201	- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
202	  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
203	  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
204	  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
205	- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
206	  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
207	- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
208	  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
209	  any late bugfix churn there.
210	
211	## What done looks like
212	
213	- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
214	  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
215	- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
216	  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
217	  profiles) updated in place.
218	- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
219	  their selecting dispatch migrated.
220	- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
221	- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
222	- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
223	  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
224	  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
225	
226	## Out of scope
227	
228	- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
229	  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
230	- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
231	  by decision above.
232	- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
233	- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.
234	
235	## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)
236	
237	Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:
238	
239	1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
240	   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
241	   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
242	   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
243	2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
244	   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
245	3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
246	   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
247	   graft cloud build.
